title: Chip cut
subtitle: Three converging cuts that lift a triangular chip from the wood surface
excerpt: The chip cut is the foundation of chip carving: three cuts converging to lift a small triangular prism of wood cleanly from the surface. Every decorative pattern in chip carving is built from repetitions of this single operation. This technique covers the three-cut sequence, the depth rules, grain direction across the six possible orientations, and the strop cadence that keeps the cuts clean.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for chip-carving in treen and small domestic woodwork. Early-twentieth-century Swedish slöjd manuals for the three-cut chip sequence on flat surfaces.

glossaryTerms.definition: Three converging cuts that lift a small triangular chip from the wood surface. The foundation of chip carving.
A short vertical cut into the wood that sets the boundary a subsequent cut must meet. Prevents grain-tearing past the intended end-point.

content.text: Chip carving builds geometric patterns from a single repeated unit: the
chip cut
. Each chip is a triangular prism of wood lifted cleanly from the surface by three cuts: a
stop cut

at the deepest point and two angled slicing cuts from the base corners to meet it. Mastered, the chip cut can be placed precisely in any orientation on a flat surface, and rows of chips in different orientations build up the borders, rosettes, and interlocking patterns that characterise the tradition.
What it does

A chip cut removes a precise triangular volume from the surface, leaving three smooth faceted walls meeting at a central low point. The surrounding surface is untouched. Repeated across a surface, chip cuts produce a pattern of shadows and facets: the inside of each chip is 1 to 3 mm below the original surface and the walls catch the light differently from the flat areas between them.
When to use it

On flat or gently curved surfaces of close-grained hardwoods: spoon handles, box lids, picture-frame faces, the flat face of a spatula handle. Suitable woods: sycamore, birch, beech, lime, cherry. Avoid open-grained species (ash, oak at the growth-ring boundaries) where the chip walls will tear at the grain direction changes. Work on seasoned, dry, lightly oiled wood only.
What you need

A sloyd knife or dedicated chip-carving knife, very sharp: sharper than for any other carving operation. A leather strop. The strop cadence for chip carving is every five chips; any duller and the cuts tear. A pencil for grid marking.
Step-by-step
Step 1: Mark the triangle

Draw an equilateral triangle with 8 mm sides on the work surface in pencil. Mark a small dot at the apex (deepest point). The triangle should be aligned with or against the grain: along the grain the stop cut is easiest; across the grain the slicing cuts are easier. For a first attempt, orient the triangle with the apex pointing along the grain direction.
Step 2: Stop cut at the apex

Press the knife point straight down at the apex dot to a depth of 2 to 3 mm, perpendicular to the surface. This is the stop cut. Do not rock the blade; keep it vertical. Withdraw the blade cleanly. This cut sets the bottom boundary that both slicing cuts must reach. Without it, the slicing cuts overtake the apex and split the ridge between adjacent chips.
Step 3: First slicing cut from a base corner

Place the blade at the left base corner of the triangle, angled at forty-five degrees to the surface and pointed toward the apex. Draw the blade toward the apex in a smooth slicing motion, angling slightly under the surface so the blade reaches 2 to 3 mm deep at the apex and emerges at the surface at the base corner. The cut is along the grain in the typical orientation; it should peel cleanly.
Step 4: Second slicing cut from the other base corner

Mirror Step 3 from the right base corner. As this cut meets the first and the stop cut at the apex, the chip lifts and falls. If it does not lift, a shallow third pass connecting the two base corners releases it. Do not force the chip out; if it resists, a third cut is quicker and cleaner than levering.
What it looks like when it is right

Three clean flat walls, smooth to the touch, meeting at a sharp point at the apex. The base of the chip is slightly concave where the two slicing cuts met below the surface. The ridges between chips are sharp and unbroken. The whole chip is 1 to 3 mm deep; shallower chips are possible but harder to see in natural light.
Common mistakes
Skipping the stop cut: the slicing cuts race past the apex and split the ridge, ruining the geometry of the chip and the one next to it.
Wrong grain orientation: if the slicing cut is going against the grain, the chip wall tears instead of cutting cleanly. Rotate the triangle ninety degrees and retry.
Chips deeper than 4 mm: the walls become unstable and the ridges between chips are thin enough to snap under the knife. Keep chips to 2 to 3 mm on thin workpieces.
